abstract: This article evaluates the interpretive framework for reality popularized by Keith Ward, arguing that Ward utilizes specific panentheistic criteria to bridge material and spiritual understandings of existence. Analyzing this position from a Reformational theological perspective, the study explores the underlying reasons why panentheism and a bipolar conception of God have gained significant traction within certain Christian circles. It further examines why these views are increasingly perceived as compelling alternatives to classical theism. Finally, the article offers critical recommendations for those navigating the intellectual appeal of generic Christian panentheism, particularly when it threatens to eclipse traditional doctrines of God.
